isoTitle: Understanding the Organization and Its Context
cbeTitle: 1.1 – Cybersecurity Governance Framework / 2.1 – Cybersecurity Risk Identification
rationale: CBE 1.1 (Governance Framework) and 2.1 (Risk Identification) partially address organizational context analysis. No direct PCI DSS equivalent exists for ISMS context determination.

isoTitle: Understanding Needs and Expectations of Interested Parties
cbeTitle: 1.1 – Cybersecurity Governance Framework / 2.4 – Third-Party & Outsourcing Risk Management
rationale: CBE 1.1 (Governance) and 2.4 (Third-Party Risk) partially address stakeholder requirements. PCI DSS does not cover the broader interested-party concept.

isoTitle: Determining the Scope of the ISMS
cbeTitle: 1.1 – Cybersecurity Governance Framework / 1.3 – Cybersecurity Strategy & Policy
rationale: CBE 1.1 and 1.3 define governance and policy boundaries. PCI DSS scope is limited to the cardholder data environment, not the broader ISMS scope.

cbeTitle: 1.1 – Cybersecurity Governance Framework / 1.2 – Board & Senior Management Oversight / 1.3 – Cybersecurity Strategy & Policy
pciTitle: 12.1 – Comprehensive Information Security Policy / 12.4 – PCI DSS Compliance Managed
rationale: CBE Domain 1 (1.1, 1.2, 1.3) establishes the ISMS governance framework. PCI DSS 12.1 and 12.4 provide aligned coverage for policy and compliance management.

pciTitle: 12.1 – Comprehensive Information Security Policy
rationale: CBE 1.2 explicitly requires board-level oversight and senior management commitment. PCI DSS 12.1 requires a management-approved IS policy.

pciTitle: 12.1 – Comprehensive Information Security Policy
rationale: CBE 1.3 requires an approved and communicated IS strategy and policy. PCI DSS 12.1 directly requires a comprehensive, current, and distributed IS policy.

pciTitle: 12.4 – PCI DSS Compliance Managed
rationale: CBE 1.4 requires defined IS roles and responsibilities organization-wide. PCI DSS 12.4 covers compliance management roles and executive accountability.

isoTitle: Actions to Address Risks and Opportunities
cbeTitle: 2.1 – Cybersecurity Risk Identification / 2.2 – Cybersecurity Risk Treatment
pciTitle: 12.3 – Risks to the CDE Formally Identified, Evaluated & Managed
rationale: CBE 2.1 (risk identification) and 2.2 (risk treatment) address risks and opportunities management. PCI DSS 12.3 covers formal risk management for the cardholder data environment.

pciTitle: 12.3 – Risks to the CDE Formally Identified, Evaluated & Managed
rationale: CBE 2.1 requires a formal risk assessment methodology with defined criteria. PCI DSS 12.3 requires an annual formal risk assessment for the CDE.

cbeTitle: 2.2 – Cybersecurity Risk Treatment / 2.3 – Cybersecurity Risk Acceptance
pciTitle: 12.3 – Risks to the CDE Formally Identified, Evaluated & Managed
rationale: CBE 2.2 (treatment) and 2.3 (acceptance) address risk treatment planning and residual risk decisions. PCI DSS 12.3 covers CDE risk treatment and targeted risk analysis.

isoTitle: IS Objectives and Planning to Achieve Them
cbeTitle: 1.2 – Board & Senior Management Oversight / 2.1 – Cybersecurity Risk Identification
rationale: CBE 1.2 (management oversight) and 2.1 (risk objectives) partially address IS objectives. No direct PCI DSS equivalent exists for ISMS objectives planning.

pciTitle: 6.5 – Changes to System Components Managed Securely
rationale: CBE 13.3 addresses formal change management for systems and processes. PCI DSS 6.5 requires security controls to be maintained when changes are made to all system components.
CBE 1.2 requires management to allocate adequate resources for cybersecurity programs. No direct PCI DSS equivalent exists for ISMS resource allocation.

cbeTitle: 12.1 – Security Awareness Program / 12.2 – Specialized Security Training
pciTitle: 12.6 – Security Awareness Education Program
rationale: CBE 12.1 and 12.2 address competence requirements and training. PCI DSS 12.6 covers security awareness but does not require formal competence assessment.

pciTitle: 12.6 – Security Awareness Education Program
rationale: CBE 12.1 directly requires ongoing security awareness programs for all personnel. PCI DSS 12.6 directly requires periodic security awareness education and acknowledgment.

cbeTitle: 10.1 – Incident Response Plan / 1.3 – Cybersecurity Strategy & Policy
rationale: CBE 10.1 (IR communication) and 1.3 (policy communication) partially address ISMS communication requirements. PCI DSS has no direct equivalent.

pciTitle: 12.1 – Comprehensive Information Security Policy
rationale: CBE 1.3 (policy documentation) and 13.3 (change control) partially address documented information control. PCI DSS 12.1 requires maintaining a current, accessible IS policy.

cbeTitle: 1.1 – Cybersecurity Governance Framework / 2.2 – Cybersecurity Risk Treatment
pciTitle: 12.4 – PCI DSS Compliance Managed
rationale: CBE 1.1 (governance) and 2.2 (risk treatment) address operational planning and control of security processes. PCI DSS 12.4 covers operational compliance management.

pciTitle: 12.3 – Risks to the CDE Formally Identified, Evaluated & Managed
rationale: CBE 2.1 requires periodic risk assessments at planned intervals. PCI DSS 12.3 requires at least an annual formal risk assessment for the cardholder data environment.

cbeTitle: 2.2 – Cybersecurity Risk Treatment / 2.3 – Cybersecurity Risk Acceptance
pciTitle: 12.3 – Risks to the CDE Formally Identified, Evaluated & Managed
rationale: CBE 2.2 and 2.3 address implementation of risk treatment plans and formal risk acceptance decisions. PCI DSS 12.3 covers CDE risk treatment and targeted risk analysis.

cbeTitle: 14.2 – Audit & Assessment / 9.3 – Security Information & Event Management (SIEM)
pciTitle: 10.4 – Audit Logs Reviewed to Identify Anomalies / 10.7 – Failures of Critical Security Controls Detected & Reported
rationale: CBE 14.2 (audit) and 9.3 (SIEM) address performance measurement and evaluation. PCI DSS 10.4 (log review) and 10.7 (control failures) partially cover monitoring requirements.

pciTitle: 11.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Security Tests & Monitoring
rationale: CBE 14.2 directly requires internal audit and assessment activities at planned intervals. PCI DSS 11.1 covers security testing processes including internal vulnerability scanning.

cbeTitle: 1.2 – Board & Senior Management Oversight / 14.2 – Audit & Assessment
pciTitle: 12.4 – PCI DSS Compliance Managed
rationale: CBE 1.2 (board oversight) and 14.2 (audit results reporting) together address management review. PCI DSS 12.4 covers executive review of compliance management.

pciTitle: 12.4 – PCI DSS Compliance Managed
rationale: CBE 2.2 (risk treatment improvement) and 14.2 (audit findings) support continual improvement of the ISMS. PCI DSS 12.4 covers ongoing compliance management and improvement.

cbeTitle: 14.2 – Audit & Assessment / 10.5 – Post-Incident Review & Lessons Learned
pciTitle: 12.10 – Suspected & Confirmed Security Incidents Responded To
rationale: CBE 14.2 (corrective actions from audits) and 10.5 (lessons learned) address nonconformity handling. PCI DSS 12.10 covers incident response and post-incident corrective actions.

pciTitle: 12.1 – Comprehensive Information Security Policy
rationale: CBE 1.3 directly requires an approved, communicated IS strategy and policy. PCI DSS 12.1 requires a comprehensive management-approved IS policy reviewed at least annually.

pciTitle: 12.4 – PCI DSS Compliance Managed
rationale: CBE 1.4 directly addresses defining and assigning IS roles organization-wide. PCI DSS 12.4 covers compliance management roles including executive accountability for PCI DSS.
CBE 1.4 addresses segregation of duties through defined roles and responsibilities. PCI DSS does not explicitly require segregation of duties outside account and access management contexts.

cbeTitle: 1.2 – Board & Senior Management Oversight / 1.4 – Cybersecurity Roles & Responsibilities
pciTitle: 12.1 – Comprehensive Information Security Policy
rationale: CBE 1.2 addresses management responsibilities for ensuring personnel comply with security policies. PCI DSS 12.1 requires management commitment and approval of the IS policy.
CBE 14.1 addresses maintaining contact and compliance with regulatory authorities including the Central Bank of Egypt. PCI DSS has no direct equivalent requirement.
CBE 14.1 covers engagement with regulatory bodies and alignment with international cybersecurity frameworks. PCI DSS has no direct equivalent for special interest group engagement.

pciTitle: 12.3 – Risks to the CDE Formally Identified, Evaluated & Managed
rationale: CBE 2.1 (risk identification) includes collecting and analysing threat intelligence. PCI DSS 12.3 requires risk management that accounts for emerging threats.

pciTitle: 12.3 – Risks to the CDE Formally Identified, Evaluated & Managed
rationale: CBE 2.1 (risk assessment) and 13.3 (change management) address integrating security into project management. PCI DSS 12.3 covers risk management for CDE-impacting projects.

isoTitle: Inventory of information and other associated assets
pciTitle: 12.5 – PCI DSS Scope Documented & Validated
rationale: CBE 3.1 and 3.2 directly address maintaining a comprehensive inventory of assets with designated owners. PCI DSS 12.5 requires documenting and validating the CDE scope.

isoTitle: Acceptable use of information and other associated assets
pciTitle: 12.2 – Acceptable Use Policies for Technologies
rationale: CBE 3.3 (data classification and handling) addresses acceptable use policies for information assets. PCI DSS 12.2 requires acceptable use policies specifically for critical technologies.
CBE 3.2 (asset ownership) addresses accountability for assets including their return upon personnel changes or termination. PCI DSS has no direct equivalent.
CBE 3.3 directly addresses data classification schemes for sensitive and customer data within CBE-regulated entities.
CBE 3.3 includes information labelling requirements as part of the data classification and handling controls.

pciTitle: 4.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Protecting Data in Transit
rationale: CBE 3.3 (data handling) and 7.3 (encryption in transit) address information transfer rules and secure transmission. PCI DSS 4.1 requires protecting cardholder data during transmission over open public networks.

pciTitle: 1.3 – Network Access Restriction to CDE
rationale: CBE 4.1 directly addresses access control policy based on least-privilege principles. PCI DSS 1.3 requires restricting direct public access to components in the cardholder data environment.

pciTitle: 8.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Identifying Users & Authenticating Access
rationale: CBE 4.2 directly addresses identity and user account management throughout the full lifecycle. PCI DSS 8.1 requires formal processes for managing user identification and authentication.

pciTitle: 8.2 – User Identification & Authentication for Users & Admins
rationale: CBE 4.5 directly addresses password and authentication information management. PCI DSS 8.2 requires unique user identification and proper authentication for all users.

pciTitle: 7.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Restricting Access to System Components
rationale: CBE 4.2 (account management) and 4.3 (privileged access) address provisioning, reviewing, and revoking access rights. PCI DSS 7.1 requires formal access control processes for system components and data.

pciTitle: 12.8 – Third-Party Service Providers (TPSPs) Managed
rationale: CBE 2.4 directly addresses managing cybersecurity risks from third-party suppliers. PCI DSS 12.8 requires comprehensive management of all third-party service providers with CDE access.

isoTitle: Addressing information security within supplier agreements
pciTitle: 12.8 – Third-Party Service Providers (TPSPs) Managed
rationale: CBE 2.4 includes embedding security requirements in supplier agreements with right-to-audit clauses. PCI DSS 12.8 requires written agreements with TPSPs acknowledging their responsibility for CDE security.

isoTitle: Managing information security in the ICT supply chain
rationale: CBE 2.4 includes ICT supply chain security within third-party risk management. PCI DSS does not directly address ICT supply chain security as a distinct control.

isoTitle: Monitoring, review and change management of supplier services
pciTitle: 12.8 – Third-Party Service Providers (TPSPs) Managed
rationale: CBE 2.4 requires periodic monitoring and review of supplier security practices. PCI DSS 12.8 requires ongoing monitoring of TPSP compliance with applicable PCI DSS requirements.

isoTitle: Information security for use of cloud services
rationale: CBE 2.4 (third-party risk) and 5.1 (network architecture) address security requirements for cloud service adoption. PCI DSS does not treat cloud services as a distinct control domain.

isoTitle: Information security incident management planning and preparation
pciTitle: 12.10 – Suspected & Confirmed Security Incidents Responded To
rationale: CBE 10.1 directly addresses incident response planning — defining roles, responsibilities, and procedures. PCI DSS 12.10 requires a tested and approved incident response plan.

isoTitle: Assessment and decision on information security events
pciTitle: 12.10 – Suspected & Confirmed Security Incidents Responded To
rationale: CBE 10.2 directly addresses incident classification and decision-making procedures for security events. PCI DSS 12.10 includes event assessment as part of the incident response plan requirements.

pciTitle: 12.10 – Suspected & Confirmed Security Incidents Responded To
rationale: CBE 10.3 directly addresses incident containment, eradication, and recovery procedures. PCI DSS 12.10 requires documented and tested incident response procedures covering breach scenarios.
CBE 10.5 directly addresses post-incident reviews and implementing improvements from lessons learned. PCI DSS does not explicitly require a post-incident lessons-learned process.
CBE 10.3 includes evidence collection and preservation as part of incident handling procedures. PCI DSS does not explicitly require forensic evidence management.
CBE 11.1 (business continuity strategy) includes maintaining IS controls during disruption events. PCI DSS does not address information security specifically during business disruptions.
CBE 11.3 (disaster recovery planning) addresses ICT readiness and recovery procedures for business continuity. PCI DSS does not explicitly require ICT readiness planning as a distinct control.

isoTitle: Legal, statutory, regulatory and contractual requirements
rationale: CBE 14.1 directly addresses identifying and complying with legal, regulatory, and contractual requirements applicable to CBE-regulated entities.
CBE 14.1 includes intellectual property rights within its regulatory compliance requirements.

pciTitle: 3.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Protecting Stored Account Data
rationale: CBE 3.3 (data handling) and 14.3 (data privacy) address protecting records from loss, destruction, and unauthorized access. PCI DSS 3.1 requires protecting stored account data with retention and disposal policies.
CBE 14.3 directly addresses data privacy and protection of personal information per Egyptian banking regulations. PCI DSS focuses on cardholder data, not general PII privacy.

pciTitle: 11.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Security Tests & Monitoring
rationale: CBE 14.2 (audit and assessment) directly addresses independent review of IS management at planned intervals. PCI DSS 11.1 requires independent security testing processes including external assessments.

isoTitle: Compliance with policies, rules and standards for information security
rationale: CBE 14.1 (regulatory compliance) and 14.2 (audit) address periodic compliance reviews against policies and standards. PCI DSS requires compliance but does not frame it as a standalone IS control.

pciTitle: 2.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for System Components Configuration
rationale: CBE 13.3 (change management) addresses documented operating procedures for information processing. PCI DSS 2.1 requires documented processes and mechanisms for applying secure configurations.

pciTitle: 12.7 – Personnel Screened Before Hiring
rationale: CBE 12.1 addresses personnel background screening as part of the HR security program. PCI DSS 12.7 directly requires background verification checks prior to hiring personnel with CDE access.
CBE 12.1 addresses security terms and conditions of employment through awareness programs and policy acknowledgment. PCI DSS has no direct equivalent for employment security terms.

isoTitle: Information security awareness, education and training
cbeTitle: 12.1 – Security Awareness Program / 12.2 – Specialized Security Training
pciTitle: 12.6 – Security Awareness Education Program
rationale: CBE 12.1 (awareness program) and 12.2 (specialized training) directly address security awareness and education for all personnel. PCI DSS 12.6 requires periodic security awareness education and acknowledgment.
CBE 12.1 addresses disciplinary processes through security policy compliance and the awareness program. PCI DSS has no direct equivalent disciplinary control.

isoTitle: Responsibilities after termination or change of employment
rationale: CBE 12.1 addresses post-employment security responsibilities within HR security procedures and policy acknowledgment.
CBE 12.1 addresses confidentiality requirements through security awareness programs and policy acknowledgment forms.
CBE 4.6 directly addresses remote working security — requiring encrypted channels, strong authentication, and session monitoring. PCI DSS has no standalone remote working control.
CBE 10.1 (IR plan) and 9.4 (detection and alerting) address mechanisms for personnel to report security events. PCI DSS does not have a standalone event reporting control for all personnel.

pciTitle: 9.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Restricting Physical Access
rationale: CBE 8.1 (physical security policy) and 8.2 (data center access) address physical security perimeters and controlled zones. PCI DSS 9.1 requires physical security policies and controls for facilities containing system components.

pciTitle: 9.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Restricting Physical Access
rationale: CBE 8.2 directly addresses physical entry controls — MFA, mantrap, visitor registration, and CCTV. PCI DSS 9.1 and 9.2 require controlling and monitoring physical access to CDE facilities.
CBE 8.2 addresses securing offices and rooms through physical access controls, monitoring, and visitor management.

pciTitle: 9.2 – Physical Access Controls for CDE
rationale: CBE 8.2 includes continuous CCTV monitoring of premises and physical access logs. PCI DSS 9.2 requires monitoring and controlling physical access to the cardholder data environment.

isoTitle: Protecting against physical and environmental threats
rationale: CBE 8.3 (environmental controls) addresses protecting against physical and environmental threats such as fire, flood, and power failure.
CBE 8.2 addresses working in secure areas through physical access controls, monitoring, and clean desk policies.
CBE 8.4 directly addresses clean desk and clear screen policies to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
CBE 8.3 (environmental controls) addresses equipment siting and physical protection within data center facilities.
CBE 3.2 (asset ownership) addresses accountability for and protection of assets used or stored off-premises.

pciTitle: 9.4 – Media with Cardholder Data Secured
rationale: CBE 3.4 directly addresses media management — secure handling, transport, and destruction. PCI DSS 9.4 requires secure storage, access control, distribution, and destruction of media containing cardholder data.
CBE 8.3 (environmental controls) addresses supporting utilities including UPS systems, generators, and redundant power supplies.
CBE 8.3 addresses physical protection of infrastructure including cabling security within data center facilities.
CBE 8.3 (environmental controls) addresses equipment maintenance procedures to ensure ongoing availability and integrity.

isoTitle: Secure disposal or re-use of equipment
rationale: CBE 3.4 (media disposal) addresses secure disposal and sanitization of equipment containing sensitive data.
CBE 3.2 (asset ownership) addresses accountability for and protection of user endpoint devices as information assets.

pciTitle: 8.5 – Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Systems Configured Securely
rationale: CBE 4.3 directly addresses privileged access management — controlling, monitoring, and auditing all privileged access. PCI DSS 8.5 requires strict management of application and system accounts.

pciTitle: 7.3 – Access to System Components & Data Controlled via Access Control System
rationale: CBE 4.1 (access control policy) addresses restricting information access based on least-privilege principles. PCI DSS 7.3 requires an access control system for managing cardholder data access.

cbeTitle: 4.1 – Access Control Policy / 13.1 – Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC)
rationale: CBE 4.1 (access control) and 13.1 (secure SDLC) address managing and restricting access to source code and development resources.

pciTitle: 8.2 – User Identification & Authentication for Users & Admins
rationale: CBE 4.4 (MFA) and 4.5 (password management) address implementing secure authentication mechanisms. PCI DSS 8.2 requires unique user identification and proper authentication for all system components.
CBE 6.4 (security configuration management) addresses capacity management as part of system configuration and operational controls.

pciTitle: 5.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Protecting Against Malicious Software / 5.2 – Malicious Software (Malware) Prevention & Detection
rationale: CBE 6.1 (vulnerability management) and 6.4 (configuration management) address deploying and managing malware protection. PCI DSS 5.1 and 5.2 require anti-malware deployment and management on all applicable system components.

pciTitle: 6.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Developing & Maintaining Systems / 6.3 – Security Vulnerabilities Identified & Addressed
rationale: CBE 6.1 (vulnerability management) and 6.2 (patch management) directly address identifying and remediating technical vulnerabilities. PCI DSS 6.1 and 6.3 require vulnerability identification and timely remediation.

pciTitle: 1.5 – Risks from Computing Devices Connecting to Untrusted Networks / 2.2 – System Components Configured & Managed Securely
rationale: CBE 6.4 directly addresses configuration management — establishing and enforcing security baselines. PCI DSS 1.5 and 2.2 require secure configuration standards for all system components.

cbeTitle: 3.3 – Data Classification & Handling / 3.4 – Secure Media Disposal
pciTitle: 3.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Protecting Stored Account Data
rationale: CBE 3.3 (data handling) and 3.4 (media disposal) address secure deletion of information no longer required. PCI DSS 3.1 requires minimizing stored account data and implementing secure deletion policies.

cbeTitle: 7.4 – Encryption at Rest / 3.3 – Data Classification & Handling
pciTitle: 3.2 – Storage of Account Data Minimized
rationale: CBE 7.4 (encryption at rest) and 3.3 (data classification) address data masking for sensitive information. PCI DSS 3.2 requires minimizing storage of account data and rendering it unreadable where stored.

cbeTitle: 3.3 – Data Classification & Handling / 9.3 – Security Information & Event Management (SIEM)
pciTitle: 11.5 – Network Intrusions & Unexpected File Changes Detected & Responded To
rationale: CBE 3.3 (data handling) and 9.3 (SIEM) address data leakage prevention through monitoring and technical controls. PCI DSS 11.5 requires network intrusion detection and prevention systems.

cbeTitle: 11.1 – Business Continuity Strategy / 11.3 – Disaster Recovery Planning
rationale: CBE 11.1 (business continuity strategy) and 11.3 (disaster recovery planning) address information backup and recovery. PCI DSS does not require information backup as a standalone security control.
CBE 11.1 (business continuity strategy) addresses redundancy of information processing facilities to ensure availability.

cbeTitle: 9.1 – Security Event Logging / 9.2 – Log Management & Retention
pciTitle: 10.1 – Processes & Mechanisms for Logging & Monitoring All Access / 10.2 – Audit Logs Capture All Individual User Access
rationale: CBE 9.1 (security event logging) and 9.2 (log management) directly address logging requirements, retention, and protection. PCI DSS 10.1 and 10.2 require comprehensive audit logging of all access to system components.

cbeTitle: 9.3 – Security Information & Event Management (SIEM) / 9.4 – Detection & Alerting
pciTitle: 10.4 – Audit Logs Reviewed to Identify Anomalies / 11.5 – Network Intrusions & Unexpected File Changes Detected & Responded To
rationale: CBE 9.3 (SIEM) and 9.4 (detection and alerting) directly address monitoring for anomalous behaviour. PCI DSS 10.4 and 11.5 require log review, anomaly detection, and intrusion detection.

pciTitle: 10.6 – Time-Synchronization Mechanisms
rationale: CBE 9.1 (security event logging) includes clock synchronization to ensure accurate and consistent log timestamps. PCI DSS 10.6 directly requires time-synchronization technology across all system components.
CBE 4.3 (privileged access management) addresses controlling and auditing the use of privileged utility programs.

isoTitle: Installation of software on operational systems
rationale: CBE 13.3 (change management) and 6.4 (configuration management) address controls over software installation on operational systems.

cbeTitle: 5.1 – Network Architecture & Security / 5.2 – Network Perimeter Security / 5.3 – Network Segmentation & Isolation
pciTitle: 1.1 – Network Security Controls – Processes & Mechanisms / 1.2 – Network Security Controls – Configuration
rationale: CBE 5.1 (network architecture), 5.2 (perimeter security), and 5.3 (segmentation) comprehensively address network security management. PCI DSS 1.1 and 1.2 require documented and properly configured network security controls.

cbeTitle: 5.1 – Network Architecture & Security / 5.5 – Internet & Email Security
pciTitle: 1.1 – Network Security Controls – Processes & Mechanisms / 1.4 – Network Connections Between Trusted & Untrusted Networks
rationale: CBE 5.1 (network architecture) and 5.5 (internet and email security) address security of network services and protocols. PCI DSS 1.1 and 1.4 require controlling connections between trusted and untrusted networks.

pciTitle: 1.1 – Network Security Controls – Processes & Mechanisms / 1.3 – Network Access Restriction to CDE
rationale: CBE 5.3 directly addresses network segmentation and isolation of systems and environments. PCI DSS 1.1 and 1.3 require restricting direct public access to the cardholder data environment.

pciTitle: 5.3 – Anti-Malware Mechanisms Active & Maintained / 5.4 – Anti-Phishing Mechanisms
rationale: CBE 5.5 (internet and email security) addresses web filtering and blocking malicious content. PCI DSS 5.3 requires active anti-malware mechanisms and 5.4 requires anti-phishing controls.

cbeTitle: 7.1 – Cryptographic Policy / 7.3 – Encryption in Transit / 7.4 – Encryption at Rest
pciTitle: 3.2 – Storage of Account Data Minimized / 4.2 – PAN Protected with Strong Cryptography During Transmission
rationale: CBE 7.1 (policy), 7.3 (in transit), and 7.4 (at rest) comprehensively address cryptography use. PCI DSS 3.2 and 4.2 require strong cryptography for stored and transmitted cardholder data.

pciTitle: 6.2 – Bespoke & Custom Software Developed Securely
rationale: CBE 13.1 directly addresses establishing and applying a secure development lifecycle. PCI DSS 6.2 requires bespoke and custom software to be developed using secure development practices.

cbeTitle: 13.1 – Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) / 13.2 – Application Security Testing
pciTitle: 6.2 – Bespoke & Custom Software Developed Securely / 6.3 – Security Vulnerabilities Identified & Addressed
rationale: CBE 13.1 (secure SDLC) and 13.2 (application security testing) address application security requirements throughout the lifecycle. PCI DSS 6.2 and 6.3 require secure development and vulnerability identification.

isoTitle: Secure system architecture and engineering principles
pciTitle: 6.2 – Bespoke & Custom Software Developed Securely
rationale: CBE 13.1 (secure SDLC) addresses secure system architecture and engineering principles within the development process. PCI DSS 6.2 requires applying secure design principles for bespoke and custom software.

pciTitle: 6.2 – Bespoke & Custom Software Developed Securely
rationale: CBE 13.1 addresses secure coding standards as a mandatory element of the secure development lifecycle. PCI DSS 6.2 requires applying secure coding practices for all bespoke and custom software.

isoTitle: Security testing in development and acceptance
pciTitle: 6.3 – Security Vulnerabilities Identified & Addressed
rationale: CBE 13.2 directly addresses application security testing — SAST, DAST, and code review. PCI DSS 6.3 requires identifying and protecting against security vulnerabilities in all software.

cbeTitle: 2.4 – Third-Party & Outsourcing Risk Management / 13.1 – Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC)
rationale: CBE 2.4 (third-party risk) and 13.1 (secure SDLC) address security requirements for outsourced development activities. PCI DSS does not treat outsourced development as a standalone control.

isoTitle: Separation of development, test and production environments
cbeTitle: 13.1 – Secure Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC) / 13.3 – Change Management
rationale: CBE 13.1 (secure SDLC) and 13.3 (change management) address separation of development, test, and production environments.

pciTitle: 6.5 – Changes to System Components Managed Securely
rationale: CBE 13.3 directly addresses formal change management procedures for all system and infrastructure changes. PCI DSS 6.5 requires that security controls are not impacted when changes are made to system components.
CBE 13.1 (secure SDLC) addresses test information management and protection of sensitive data used in test environments.

isoTitle: Protection of information systems during audit testing
rationale: CBE 14.2 (audit and assessment) addresses protecting operational systems and minimizing disruption during audit testing activities.
